policy_statement: A8: Protect ventilation corridors through Budapest’s Settlement Structure Plan and Rules of Urban Planning by not supporting new buildable area designation or increased building heights in these corridors; compile a manual of good district examples to support climate-protection-related planning instrument review (continuous 2021–2030).
mechanism_description: It protects ventilation corridors by not supporting added buildable areas or taller buildings in those corridors, helping maintain urban airflow that reduces heat and stagnant-air impacts.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (heat + stagnant air/air pollution episodes) → urban design preserves airflow → ↓ local heat accumulation + ↓ pollutant stagnation → ↓ exposure/vulnerability
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human activity/system changed: Land-use and zoning controls limit new developable areas, building heights, and massing in designated ventilation corridors so that wind pathways through the city are not blocked.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Preserved air exchange and wind flow enhances convective cooling and disperses accumulated heat and pollutants, reducing formation/intensity of localized heat pockets and stagnant-air conditions.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Improved resilience to hotter conditions and heatwaves via lower ambient temperatures and reduced heat/air-quality health impacts (reduced exposure and sensitivity of urban residents).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing urban heat and stagnant-air exposure by maintaining ventilation pathways, thereby lowering heat-health risk during hot conditions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism acts on urban form to preserve ventilation, which directly reduces heat and stagnant-air exposure under warming conditions—an adaptation (risk-reduction) pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While maintaining airflow can indirectly reduce building cooling demand (mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefit), the direct, intended, and most generalizable effect is lowering heat and air-stagnation impacts on people and urban microclimate.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a corridor policy were justified mainly to cut energy use/CO₂ via reduced cooling loads, it could be framed as mitigation/resource efficiency; however, the mechanism as stated (heat and air quality resilience) is exposure reduction, so Adaptation dominates.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any “ventilation corridor protection” policy functions by preserving airflow to reduce heat/pollution stagnation risk, so the primary category remains Adaptation across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any secondary mitigation or air-quality co-benefits depend on local building stock, energy mix, enforcement, and meteorology and should be assessed at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations may cite mitigation co-benefits (reduced cooling energy) or general air-quality management; classification remains Adaptation unless the dominant objective/effect demonstrably shifts to GHG reduction across the mechanism’s typical applications.
key_indicators: “Protection of ventilation corridors”; “provide for the ventilation corridors”; “designation of new areas to be built up … may not be supported”; “raising of the permitted building height may not be supported”; “Settlement Structure Plan and Rules of Urban Planning”; “compile a manual”
co_benefits: Improved local air quality and public health co-benefits through reduced pollutant stagnation.
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes a secondary, softer instrument (“compile a manual”) that resembles guidance/information, but the binding land-use/height restrictions in planning documents appear to be the primary operative instrument.
